content:
6 min read
1 hour ago

--

Following one tiny DNS request through every layer of the system — from Chrome’s cache to the authoritative DNS server.
In this article we’ll follow a single DNS request through:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

DNS JourneyIf someone asked me how does my browser knows where to go, a simple engineer like me would just simply say google.com has its IP and it checks the IP address in the DNS and gets it . That’s how my browser knows where to go. Technically, that answer isn’t wrong — but it isn’t the whole story either. This tiny DNS request has quite a journey ahead of it. Along the way, it’ll meet a surprising number of characters before anyone can finally answer one simple question: “Where is google.com?”.
So the journey starts from Chrome(browser) , here is the place where we first ask the location of google.com. We don’t go straight to internet looking for the address instead browser look for its own DNS cache. But wait exactly where is this DNS cache living and who does the job of maintaining it. To understand where Chrome’s DNS cache lives, we first need to understand what happens when Chrome starts running. When we want to run the chrome executable file , OS takes over and prepares an execution environment ( checking all the ingredients before telling master chef CPU to start cooking ). This includes creating a process , allocating virtual memory for the application , map the executable , initialize stack and lot other things. For now let’s come back to question of where does the chrome has the DNS cache ? It is stored inside its own process’s virtual address space and is being maintained by chrome itself. The DNS cache has a look up table which maps the domain names to their corresponding IP , TTL (time to live) based on which we decide if the cache is usable or stale. If we find the IP in the usable cache then we move ahead with the address and journey surprisingly ends here. But if the condition is not suitable and we have a cache miss then we have to go to OS.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

DNS LookupChrome was not able to find the right address so it hands the lookup to the OS DNS resolver. DNS resolver then checks for the valid mapping in its own cache and then is checks for the etc/hosts file where we sometimes manually map a domain name to address ( remember the localhost ). If the address is not found anywhere then the resolver constructs a DNS query in memory which is then sent to kernel via a syscall. Before we move forward we should know that all the application run in the user space ( this has all the memory allocations , perform computations ) but it doesn’t have the access to the hardware to run any kind of operations. For this we need to ask kernel for performing some operations on our behalf. So upon invoking the kernel using sendto() syscall , the control is being handed over to kernel and it connects with the network hardware to communicate . At this point, the DNS query exists only as an Application Layer message. It’s simply a structured sequence of bytes sitting in memory.
Before sending the DNS query to internet kernel needs to create some layers over the query message for the components up ahead to travel. So the kernel adds a UDP header to the DNS query message to create a UDP segment ( this is the Transport layer ). Then to the UDP segment an IP Header is added to create a IP packet ( this is the network layer ). Now currently we have a IP packet ( IP header + UDP segment ( UDP header + DNS query message)) but we need to pass this to the router. We know the destination we need to pass the message through the configured DNS resolver ( 8.8.8.8 , is it ?). Now let me ask you a question how should I know where to send those packets , we know that it is supposed to go to router but how would kernel know the address of the router and it is supposed to send the packet there. We get the address from the routing table which tells the kernel for the destination (8.8.8.8) we need to first send it to the router (192.168.0.1 generally a default gateway).
Press enter or click to view image in full size

ROUTER and ARPTill Now kernel has the IP packet and destination IP to which the packet needs to be send. But the IP packet needs to be converted to ethernet frames for which we need the MAC address of the router. Here comes another concept i.e ARP ( Address Resolution Protocol )( this helps in getting kernel know about the identity ). Kernel first checks the ARP cache and after miss broadcast a ARP request asking does anyone has the information about this IP (192.168.0.1) and we get the response in form of MAC address from the router. Now kernel can form the Ethernet frame using the MAC address ( this is the data link layer ). Now kernel hands over the ethernet frame to the network interface card NIC which has the responsibility of sending the message in electric signals or radio waves.
Now we have finally reached the router, to recap we came from
browser (checking its cache and calling the OS resolver), OS (creating the DNS request and encapsulating in different layers), NIC ( handles the message and sends it to router).
Router removes the layer 2 and examines the IP packet to get the desired IP address. It also maintains a routing table and using that we get to know the next hopping address. ( One thing to keep in mind before every hop we check for the next address in the ARP cache ). We again do the above process and transfer to next hop.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Recursive DNS resolverOur request has finally reached the configured recursive DNS resolver. This is the first machine in our journey whose responsibility is to answer the question rather than simply forward the packet. Before contacting anyone else, the recursive resolver checks its own DNS cache. If a valid record for google.com already exists, it immediately returns the IP address.
If the cache doesn't contain the answer, the resolver begins the DNS lookup. It first contacts a Root DNS server, which doesn't know Google's IP address but knows which servers manage the .com top-level domain. The resolver then queries one of the .com TLD servers, which in turn points it to Google's authoritative name servers. Finally, the resolver asks the authoritative DNS server responsible for google.com, which returns the IP address.
Before sending the response back to us, the recursive resolver stores the result in its own cache for the duration specified by the record’s TTL. This way, future requests for google.com can often be answered immediately without repeating the entire lookup.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The return trip is surprisingly uneventful. The response retraces almost the exact same path it came through. Routers don’t suddenly become smarter on the way back — they simply keep forwarding packets according to their routing tables. Every router strips the incoming Ethernet frame, examines the IP packet, builds a new Ethernet frame for the next hop, and forwards it along. Eventually the packet reaches our router, then our network interface card, then the kernel, which removes the networking headers and hands the original DNS response back to the operating system’s resolver.
The operating system may cache the result, Chrome may cache it again in its own DNS cache, and finally the browser receives the answer it has been waiting for.
At last, Chrome knows that google.com resolves to an IP address.
Only now can the browser begin the next stage of the adventure.
